market_summary: Markets are broadly flat to slightly positive today (S&P 500 ~+0.1%, Nasdaq ~+0.2%) as AI momentum continues to drive tech outperformance; Nvidia's Computex keynote and Microsoft's Build AI announcements are the dominant catalysts powering the sector.

MSFT.headline: Wells Fargo raises MSFT price target to $650 (from $625) with Overweight rating on June 1, citing Microsoft's bold AI push including homegrown models unveiled at Build and a $1B expanded AI alliance with EY.
MSFT.reasoning: Microsoft is firing on all cylinders for long-term holders: its AI-focused Build conference unveiled proprietary models, it deepened its EY enterprise AI alliance worth up to $1B, and Wells Fargo's fresh $650 PT underscores analyst conviction. Today's trading volume of 79.65M vs. the 38.42M daily average signals strong institutional interest. The TTM P/E of ~27.4x is meaningfully below its 5-year median of 34.15x, suggesting the AI re-rating is not yet complete — long-term thesis firmly intact.

AMZN.headline: AMZN pulls back modestly intraday as AWS continues to post its fastest growth in 15 quarters, with Truist raising its price target to $320 and Amazon licensing its 'Alexa for Shopping' AI tool to third-party retailers.
AMZN.reasoning: Today's -2.04% dip is routine profit-taking noise after a strong run near 52-week highs ($278.56), not a fundamental development. AWS cloud growth at its fastest pace in nearly four years and Amazon's $200B+ 2026 capex commitment to AI infrastructure are long-term positives. The next earnings catalyst is July 30, 2026 — no action warranted here; the long-term cloud and AI infrastructure thesis is intact.

NVDA.headline: Nvidia surged ~5% today as CEO Jensen Huang's Computex keynote unveiled the RTX Spark AI PC processor, expanding Nvidia's AI footprint from data centers to desktops, while Goldman Sachs reaffirmed its Buy rating.
NVDA.reasoning: The Computex keynote is a genuine positive catalyst: the new RTX Spark chip extends Nvidia's AI monetization into the consumer PC market, a new and large addressable segment, while the data center business continues to dominate. With a 52-week gain of ~66% and market cap at $5.43T, valuation is full but the competitive moat remains unmatched. Long-term holders should stay put — this is exactly the kind of product expansion that sustains multi-year compounding.

GOOGL.headline: Alphabet is pulling back from its May 18 52-week high of $408.61, down ~8% over the past two weeks, even as Piper Sandler raises its price target to $445 and analysts highlight Google as a 'clear beneficiary' of the AI search boom.
GOOGL.reasoning: The -1.28% decline and ~8% pullback from highs is worth monitoring given the stock is down 6 of the last 10 sessions, though the fundamental story remains strong: Q1 2026 revenue grew 22% to $110B with Google Cloud surging 63% to $20B. The ex-dividend date is June 8 ($0.22/share), which may be contributing to slight selling pressure. This is a WATCH — not for action, but to confirm the pullback stabilizes above the $369 long-term moving average support; no sell signal for a long-term holder.

alerts.message: GOOGL has declined ~8% from its May 18 52-week high of $408.61 and is down 6 of the last 10 sessions. Monitor for stabilization above the $369 support level; fundamentals (Cloud +63% YoY) remain intact and Piper Sandler raised its PT to $445 today — no action needed, but watch for further deterioration.

long_term_view: All four holdings remain core positions in the AI infrastructure supercycle: NVDA dominates compute, MSFT leads enterprise AI software, AMZN's AWS is accelerating cloud AI adoption, and GOOGL's Cloud and Search AI integration are both inflecting positively. Today's modest AMZN and GOOGL weakness is noise against a backdrop of extraordinary multi-year earnings power growth across the entire portfolio.
